title: Say Goodbye to Chapped Lips with 10 Best Herbal Teas
intro_desc: Herbal teas for chapped lips are a natural remedy that helps to soothe and heal dry, cracked lips by providing essential moisture and antioxidants. The best herbal teas for chapped lips include Calendula officinalis, Aloe barbadensis, Aloe vera, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Echinacea purpurea, Melissa officinalis, Symphytum officinale, Chamomilla recutita, Hibiscus sabdariffa, and Mentha x piperita. If left untreated, chapped lips can lead to discomfort, pain, and difficulty eating, speaking, or even smiling, negatively impacting daily life and causing embarrassment and self-consciousness.

remedies.remedy_desc: Aloe barbadensis tea is good for chapped lips because it has soothing properties that help heal and calm dry, irritated skin. The gel from the aloe plant is rich in vitamins A, C, and E, as well as minerals like calcium and potassium. These nutrients help to moisturize and protect the skin, reducing inflammation and redness. When used as a lip balm or applied to the lips, aloe barbadensis tea can help lock in moisture and speed up the healing process of chapped lips. It's also gentle and non-irritating, making it suitable for people with sensitive skin or allergies. Additionally, aloe barbadensis tea has antibacterial properties that help prevent infections and promote the growth of new skin cells, making it an effective remedy for chapped, cracked lips.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Anthraquinones are good for chapped lips because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and calm irritated skin, reducing redness and discomfort, allowing lips to heal and recover quickly.
Flavonoids are good for chapped lips because they have strong antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that help to heal and soothe dry, irritated skin on the lips.
Aloe-emodin is good for chapped lips because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce swelling and soothe irritation, promoting healthy and smooth lip skin.

remedies.image_alt: Aloe barbadensis herbal teas for chapped lips
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing Hibiscus sabdariffa tea that helps with chapped lips, start by gathering the dried calyxes of the plant, which are the outer flower parts that hold the seeds. Rinse the calyxes in a fine-mesh strainer under cold running water, then let them dry completely. Next, add one tablespoon of the dried calyxes to a cup of boiling water. Remove from heat, cover, and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea into another cup, then discard the solids. Let the tea cool down, and once it reaches a comfortable temperature, use it as a mouthwash by swishing it around your mouth and spitting it out. You can also use it as a lip balm by applying it directly to your lips with a cotton swab. Repeat this process 2-3 times a day to get the best results and to help soothe and heal dry, chapped lips.

remedies.remedy_desc: Calendula officinalis tea has healing properties that make it good for chapped lips. The antioxidants in calendula tea help to reduce inflammation and soothe dry, irritated skin. When applied topically to chapped lips, the tea can provide relief from pain and discomfort. Calendula tea also has antimicrobial properties, which can help to prevent infections and promote healing. As the tea absorbs into the skin, it helps to lock in moisture and protect the skin from further dryness. This can lead to faster healing and a reduction in the appearance of chapped, cracked lips. Regular use of calendula tea can also help to strengthen the skin and prevent future chapping.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Tannins are good for chapped lips because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to soothe and protect dry, irritated skin on the lips.
Quercetin is good for chapped lips because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and heal dry, cracked skin, reducing irritation and promoting healthy lip tissue.
Saponins are good for chapped lips because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and heal irritated skin, reducing dryness and discomfort.

remedies.image_alt: Calendula officinalis herbal teas for chapped lips
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing hibiscus tea that helps with chapped lips, start by boiling one cup of water in a pot. While the water is boiling, take a handful of dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers and add them to the pot. Let the flowers steep in the boiling water for about 5-7 minutes, allowing their natural goodness to release into the water. After the steeping time, carefully pour the tea into a cup using a strainer to hold back the flowers. Let the tea cool down for a few minutes before using it as a lip balm. You can apply the tea directly to your chapped lips by dipping a cotton swab into the cooled tea and gently rubbing it onto your lips. Alternatively, you can also add a tablespoon of honey to the cooled tea to create a thicker, more nourishing lip balm.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra tea is a good remedy for chapped lips because it has anti-inflammatory properties. This means it can help reduce swelling and redness in chapped lips. The tea also contains antioxidants that protect the skin from damage caused by free radicals. Glycyrrhiza glabra tea has moisturizing properties that help lock in moisture and soothe dry, cracked skin. It can also help reduce irritation and discomfort caused by chapped lips. Drinking the tea can help from the inside, while applying the cooled tea bags to the lips can provide a cooling and soothing effect, helping to heal and protect chapped lips.

remedies.image_alt: Glycyrrhiza glabra herbal teas for chapped lips
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hibiscus sabdariffa herbal tea that helps with chapped lips, start by gathering the necessary ingredients. You will need 1 cup of fresh or dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers, 1 cup of boiling water, and a cup or mug to steep the tea in. Begin by rinsing the flowers gently in cold water to remove any dirt or debris. Then, add the flowers to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea into a cup or mug, and let it cool down to a comfortable temperature. Once the tea has cooled, use it as a lip balm by applying it directly to your chapped lips with a cotton swab. The antioxidants and antibacterial properties in Hibiscus sabdariffa can help soothe and heal chapped lips, making them soft and smooth again. You can also store the cooled tea in the fridge for later use, making it a convenient and natural remedy for dry and chapped lips.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis tea, also known as lemon balm tea, has several benefits for chapped lips. The tea is rich in antioxidants that help soothe and calm irritated skin. The antibacterial properties of the tea can help prevent infections that can make chapped lips worse. The tea also contains antiseptic and anti-inflammatory compounds that reduce redness and swelling. Additionally, the tea has a calming effect, which can help reduce stress and anxiety that can cause lip irritation. When applied topically, the tea can help lock in moisture and protect the lips from the elements, helping to heal and repair dry, cracked skin. The tea can be consumed as a drink, or applied directly to the lips using a cooled tea bag or a cotton swab.

remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal teas for chapped lips
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing herbal tea from Hibiscus sabdariffa that can help with chapped lips, start by gathering the necessary ingredients: dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers, water, and a sweetener like honey if desired. Begin by boiling 1 quart of water in a large pot, then add 1/4 cup of dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers to the boiling water. Reduce the heat and let the mixture simmer for 10-15 minutes, allowing the flowers to release their medicinal properties into the water. Next, strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large container, pressing on the solids to extract as much liquid as possible. Discard the solids and let the tea cool to room temperature. Once cooled, you can store the tea in the refrigerator for up to 3 days or drink it immediately. To use the tea, soak a cotton ball or a clean cloth in the liquid and apply it to your chapped lips for 5-10 minutes, allowing the antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties of Hibiscus sabdariffa to work their way into your skin.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea tea is a natural remedy for chapped lips. The plant's active compounds, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and calm irritated skin. This can bring quick relief to dry, cracked, and painful lips. The antioxidants in Echinacea purpurea tea also protect the skin from damage caused by free radicals, which can make lips look healthier and more radiant. Additionally, the tea has antimicrobial properties that can help prevent infections in the affected area. Drinking Echinacea purpurea tea may also help boost the immune system, which can aid in the healing process of chapped lips. Applying a cooled-down Echinacea purpurea tea bag directly to the lips can help lock in moisture and provide extra hydration, helping to repair and protect dry, chapped skin.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing Hibiscus sabdariffa tea for chapped lips, start by gathering fresh or dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers, as they are the main ingredient in this herbal remedy. Rinse the flowers with clean water to remove any impurities. If using fresh flowers, gently crush or bruise them to release their active ingredients. In a medium-sized saucepan, combine the hibiscus flowers and about 2 cups of water, making sure the flowers are completely submerged. Bring the mixture to a boil and then reduce the heat to a simmer for about 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors and nutrients to infuse into the water. After the tea has cooled, strain it using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove the hibiscus flowers. Now, your Hibiscus sabdariffa tea is ready to be used. To apply it to chapped lips, simply dip a cotton swab or a soft cloth into the tea and gently rub it onto the affected area. Repeat this process a few times a day to help soothe and moisturize dry, chapped lips.

remedies.remedy_desc: Aloe vera tea can be good for chapped lips because it has soothing properties that help calm and moisturize dry skin. Aloe vera contains a compound called aloin, which reduces inflammation and eases irritation on chapped lips. Drinking aloe vera tea can also provide the body with antioxidants, which help repair damaged skin cells. As a result, the skin on your lips may feel softer and more comfortable. Additionally, aloe vera tea may help balance the body's natural pH levels, which can also contribute to healthier, less chapped lips.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing Hibiscus sabdariffa herbal tea for chapped lips, start by gathering 1 cup of dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers and 2 cups of boiling water. Pour the boiling water over the dried flowers in a heat-resistant cup or teapot, and let it steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors and nutrients to infuse. Strain the tea into a separate container to remove the flowers, and let it cool down to room temperature. Once cooled, use the tea as a natural lip balm by applying it directly to your chapped lips with a cotton swab or a clean finger. You can also add a few drops of honey or aloe vera gel to the tea for extra moisturizing benefits. Drink the remaining tea as a refreshing and healthy beverage, or store it in the refrigerator for later use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita tea, also known as peppermint tea, is a great remedy for chapped lips. This is because peppermint has anti-inflammatory properties, which help to soothe and calm the skin. When you drink peppermint tea, the menthol in it gets into your bloodstream and helps to reduce swelling and redness in the affected area. This can bring quick relief to dry, cracked, and chapped lips. The cooling sensation of peppermint tea also helps to numb the pain and discomfort caused by chapped lips, making it a more pleasant experience to have a drink. Additionally, peppermint tea has antimicrobial properties, which help to prevent infections in the cracked skin of the lips. Drinking peppermint tea regularly can also help to keep the skin hydrated and healthy, reducing the likelihood of chapped lips in the future.

Rosemary tea also has antibacterial properties, which can help prevent infections in chapped lips. When you drink rosemary tea, the antioxidants and other beneficial compounds can reach your lips through your bloodstream, helping to soothe and heal chapped skin. The tea may also help to lock in moisture, keeping your lips hydrated and soft.

First, boil about 2 cups of water in a pot and add 1-2 teaspoons of dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers. Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the flowers to release their flavor and medicinal properties. Then, strain the tea into a cup using a strainer and discard the flowers. Let the tea cool down to room temperature. Once it's cooled, you can store it in the refrigerator for later use. To use the tea as a lip treatment, dip a cotton ball or swab into the cooled tea and gently apply it to your chapped lips. Repeat this process 2-3 times a day, or as needed, to help soothe and moisturize your lips. The antioxidants and antibacterial properties in Hibiscus sabdariffa can help to reduce inflammation and promote healing of chapped lips, leaving them smooth and soft.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a soothing Hibiscus sabdariffa herbal tea that helps with chapped lips, start by gathering the right ingredients. You will need dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers, which you can find at a local health food store or online. Next, boil 1 cup of water in a pot, then remove it from heat. Add 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried Hibiscus sabdariffa flowers to the boiling water and let it steep for 5 to 10 minutes. Strain the liquid and discard the flowers. Your tea is now ready to drink. To use it for chapped lips, let the tea cool down, then soak a cotton ball or a clean cloth in it. Gently apply the soaked cloth to your chapped lips for 5 to 10 minutes. The antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties in Hibiscus sabdariffa will help soothe and heal your dry, chapped lips. Repeat this process as needed to get relief from chapped lips.

supplementary_best: To remedy chapped lips, a combination of herbal teas from 'Aloe barbadensis', 'Glycyrrhiza glabra', and 'Mentha x piperita' is the best. 'Aloe barbadensis' is rich in vitamins A, C, and E, which help soothe and moisturize dry lips. 'Glycyrrhiza glabra' or licorice root, is known for its anti-inflammatory properties that reduce redness and irritation. 'Mentha x piperita' or peppermint, has a cooling effect that calms and cools the lips, reducing the discomfort of chapped lips.  When consumed as a tea, 'Aloe barbadensis' provides hydration, 'Glycyrrhiza glabra' reduces inflammation, and 'Mentha x piperita' cools the lips, addressing the primary issues of chapped lips. The combination works synergistically, making it the best choice for this problem.
supplementary_avoid: If you have chapped lips, it's best to avoid certain herbal teas. One of these teas is sage (Salvia officinalis). This is because sage can dry out your skin and lips even further, making the problem worse. Sage is a natural astringent, which means it helps to reduce moisture in your skin. Drinking sage tea may reduce the moisture levels in your lips, making them even chapped.  Another herbal tea to avoid is chamomile tea (Matricaria chamomilla). While chamomile is known for its calming effects, it can also dry out your skin and lips. This is because chamomile tea has astringent properties, similar to sage tea. Drinking chamomile tea regularly may dry out your lips and make them more prone to chapping.  You should also avoid drinking teas made from yarrow (Achillea millefolium). Yarrow tea has a drying effect on the skin, which can make chapped lips worse. Yarrow's astringent properties can reduce moisture levels in your skin, causing dryness and irritation. Drinking yarrow tea regularly may exacerbate chapped lips.  Lastly, avoid drinking teas made from rosehip (Rosa rubiginosa) if you have chapped lips. While rosehip tea is rich in vitamins and antioxidants, it can also have a drying effect on the skin. The astringent properties in rosehip tea may reduce moisture levels in your skin, making chapped lips worse.
supplementary_other_remedies: Coconut oil is a natural remedy that can help with chapped lips. It's rich in fatty acids that lock moisture into the skin, keeping lips soft and smooth. You can apply it directly to your lips throughout the day or before bedtime. Another option is beeswax, which creates a barrier on the skin's surface to prevent moisture loss. It's often mixed with other ingredients like coconut oil or essential oils to make a balm. Aloe vera gel is also effective in soothing and hydrating dry lips. Its anti-inflammatory properties can help calm irritated skin and reduce discomfort. You can apply aloe vera gel directly to your lips or use it as a base for your homemade lip balm. Honey is another natural ingredient that can help with chapped lips. It has antibacterial properties that can help prevent infection and promote healing. Applying a thin layer of honey to your lips can provide temporary relief from dryness and discomfort.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with chapped lips may also experience dryness and irritation in other parts of their body. One common issue is dry skin on the hands and feet, which can be relieved with aloe vera. Aloe vera has moisturizing properties that help to lock in moisture and soothe dry skin.  Another common issue is a dry, itchy throat, which can be eased with licorice root. Licorice root has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce inflammation and irritation in the throat.  Some people may also experience dry, irritated eyes, which can be relieved with eyebright. Eyebright has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce swelling and soothe dry, irritated eyes.  People with chapped lips may also experience dry, itchy skin on their face, which can be relieved with calendula. Calendula has antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties that help to soothe and protect dry, irritated skin.  In addition, people with chapped lips may also experience dry, irritated nasal passages, which can be relieved with eucalyptus. Eucalyptus has decongestant properties that help to reduce congestion and soothe dry, irritated nasal passages.  These herbs can help to relieve these symptoms because they have anti-inflammatory and moisturizing properties that help to soothe and protect dry, irritated skin and mucous membranes. They can be used topically or taken as a tea or supplement to help alleviate these symptoms.
